umbrellas.label: Brønsted–Lowry Definitions & Conjugate Pairs
umbrellas.description: The Brønsted–Lowry framework defines acids and bases by proton transfer and introduces conjugate acid–base pairs.

intro.label: Brønsted–Lowry Definitions Overview
intro.description: In the Brønsted–Lowry model, an acid donates a proton ($\text{H}^+$) and a base accepts one — every acid–base reaction transfers a proton from donor to acceptor. Every acid has a conjugate base (it minus $\text{H}^+$), and every base has a conjugate acid (it plus $\text{H}^+$). This proton-transfer framework underlies pH, $K_a$/$K_b$, buffers, and titration.
examples: acid = proton donor; base = proton acceptor

in_depth.label: Brønsted–Lowry acid and base definition
in_depth.description: Identify a Brønsted–Lowry acid as a proton ($\text{H}^+$) donor and a Brønsted–Lowry base as a proton acceptor in any aqueous or non-aqueous reaction. This focuses on the proton-transfer definition, not the electron-pair Lewis definition (built in C2).

in_depth.label: Conjugate acid–base pairs
in_depth.description: Identify conjugate acid–base pairs in a proton-transfer equilibrium: the conjugate base of an acid is the acid minus one proton, and the conjugate acid of a base is the base plus one proton. This focuses on recognizing and labeling the two conjugate pairs in a reaction, not on calculating their $K_a$/$K_b$ values (separate keywords).
examples: two conjugate pairs in every proton-transfer equation

umbrellas.label: Ionization of Water & pH Scale
umbrellas.description: Water self-ionizes to give $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 10^{-14}$ at 25°C, establishing the pH and pOH scales and setting the neutral point at pH 7.

intro.label: Water Ionization and pH Overview
intro.description: Pure water autoionizes: $\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^-$, with $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 10^{-14}$ at 25°C. pH is defined as $-\log[\text{H}^+]$ and pOH as $-\log[\text{OH}^-]$, and they always sum to 14. These relationships let you convert between $[\text{H}^+]$, $[\text{OH}^-]$, pH, and pOH for any aqueous solution.

in_depth.label: Water autoionization and $K_w$
in_depth.description: Apply the water-ionization equilibrium $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 10^{-14}$ at 25°C to relate $[\text{H}^+]$ and $[\text{OH}^-]$ in any aqueous solution. This focuses on $K_w$ as a constant linking $[\text{H}^+]$ and $[\text{OH}^-]$, not on the pH formula itself (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Definition of pH and the pH scale
in_depth.description: Define $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$ and use the scale (0–14 in aqueous solution at 25°C) to classify solutions as acidic ($\text{pH} < 7$), neutral ($\text{pH} = 7$), or basic ($\text{pH} > 7$). This focuses on the definition and scale, not on calculating pH from specific acid/base concentrations (separate keywords).
examples: pH 7 = neutral; pH < 7 = acidic; pH > 7 = basic

in_depth.label: $\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = 14$
in_depth.description: Use the relation $\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = 14$ (derived from $-\log K_w$ at 25°C) to convert between pH and pOH when one is known. This focuses on the complementary relationship between pH and pOH, not on $K_w$ itself (a separate keyword).
examples: pOH of a solution at pH 11 is 3

umbrellas.label: Strong Acids & Bases
umbrellas.description: Strong acids and bases dissociate completely in water; pH is calculated directly from their molarity without an equilibrium expression.

intro.label: Strong Acids and Bases Overview
intro.description: A strong acid or base dissociates essentially 100% in dilute aqueous solution, so $[\text{H}^+]$ (or $[\text{OH}^-]$) equals the initial concentration. Common strong acids include $\text{HCl}$, $\text{HBr}$, $\text{HI}$, $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, $\text{HNO}_3$, and $\text{HClO}_4$; strong bases include group-1 hydroxides ($\text{NaOH}$, $\text{KOH}$) and barium hydroxide. Because dissociation is complete, no equilibrium constant is needed — just molarity → pH.
examples: HCl, HBr, HI, $\text{HNO}_3$, $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, $\text{HClO}_4$ = six strong acids

in_depth.label: Identifying strong acids
in_depth.description: Recognize the six common strong acids ($\text{HCl}$, $\text{HBr}$, $\text{HI}$, $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, $\text{HNO}_3$, $\text{HClO}_4$) and predict complete dissociation in dilute aqueous solution. This focuses on classification (which acids are strong), not on pH calculation from their concentrations (a separate keyword).
examples: no $K_a$ expression needed for strong acids

in_depth.label: Identifying strong bases
in_depth.description: Recognize common strong bases (group-1 metal hydroxides: $\text{LiOH}$, $\text{NaOH}$, $\text{KOH}$, $\text{RbOH}$, $\text{CsOH}$; also $\text{Ba(OH)_2}$ and $\text{Sr(OH)_2}$) and predict complete dissociation. This focuses on classification, not on pH/pOH calculation (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: pH calculation for strong acids and bases
in_depth.description: Calculate the pH of a strong acid solution from its molarity using $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$ (where $[\text{H}^+] = C_{\text{acid}}$), and calculate pH of a strong base using $[\text{OH}^-] = C_{\text{base}}$ then $\text{pH} = 14 - \text{pOH}$. This focuses on strong-acid/base pH (complete dissociation, no equilibrium), not weak-acid/base pH from $K_a$/$K_b$ (separate keywords).
examples: diprotic $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$: both protons at MCAT depth

umbrellas.label: Weak Acids & Bases — Equilibria
umbrellas.description: Weak acids and bases partially dissociate in water; their equilibrium is quantified by $K_a$, $K_b$, $\text{p}K_a$, $\text{p}K_b$, and the pH is found using an equilibrium ICE-table approach.

intro.label: Weak Acid/Base Equilibria Overview
intro.description: A weak acid ($\text{HA}$) or weak base ($\text{B}$) establishes a partial-dissociation equilibrium in water, described by $K_a$ or $K_b$. The logarithmic forms $\text{p}K_a = -\log K_a$ and $\text{p}K_b = -\log K_b$ rank acid/base strength: smaller $\text{p}K_a$ (larger $K_a$) = stronger acid. pH calculations use an ICE table or the small-$x$ approximation when $K_a \ll C$.
examples: ICE table or small-$x$ approx for pH

in_depth.label: Acid dissociation constant $K_a$
in_depth.description: Write the $K_a$ expression for a weak acid dissociation $\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$ as $K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$, and use $K_a$ magnitude to rank relative acid strength. This focuses on the definition and expression for $K_a$, not on calculating pH from it (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Base dissociation constant $K_b$
in_depth.description: Write the $K_b$ expression for a weak base $\text{B} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{BH}^+ + \text{OH}^-$ as $K_b = \frac{[\text{BH}^+][\text{OH}^-]}{[\text{B}]}$, and use the conjugate relationship $K_a \cdot K_b = K_w = 10^{-14}$ to interconvert. This focuses on $K_b$ and the conjugate relationship, not on calculating $\text{pH}$ from it (a separate keyword).
examples: stronger base → larger $K_b$, smaller $K_a$ of conjugate acid

in_depth.label: $\text{p}K_a$ and $\text{p}K_b$
in_depth.description: Convert between $K_a$ and $\text{p}K_a = -\log K_a$ (and $K_b$ and $\text{p}K_b = -\log K_b$), and use the relation $\text{p}K_a + \text{p}K_b = 14$ to interconvert a conjugate pair. This focuses on the logarithmic form and conjugate relationship, not on the Henderson–Hasselbalch application (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: pH of a weak acid solution
in_depth.description: Calculate the pH of a weak acid solution using an ICE table or the small-$x$ approximation: $[\text{H}^+] \approx \sqrt{K_a \cdot C}$ when $K_a \ll C$. This focuses on pH from weak acid dissociation only, not from a weak base or salt solution (separate keywords).

in_depth.label: pH of a weak base solution
in_depth.description: Calculate the pH of a weak base solution using $[\text{OH}^-] \approx \sqrt{K_b \cdot C}$, then convert to pH via $\text{pOH} = -\log[\text{OH}^-]$ and $\text{pH} = 14 - \text{pOH}$. This focuses on weak-base pH, not weak-acid or salt solution pH (separate keywords).

in_depth.label: Dissociation of weak acid with added salt
in_depth.description: Predict the effect of adding the conjugate base salt on a weak acid equilibrium (common-ion effect): added $\text{A}^-$ suppresses $[\text{H}^+]$ and raises pH. This focuses on the common-ion suppression of weak-acid dissociation, not on the buffer capacity formula (Henderson–Hasselbalch, a separate keyword).
examples: common-ion effect raises pH of weak acid

in_depth.label: Polyprotic acids
in_depth.description: Analyze stepwise dissociation of polyprotic acids (e.g., $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$, $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$): each step has its own $K_{a1} > K_{a2} > K_{a3}$ because removing successive protons from an increasingly negative ion is harder. This focuses on sequential dissociation and the ordering of $K_a$ values, not on single-proton weak acid pH (a separate keyword).
examples: pH determined mainly by first dissociation

umbrellas.label: Salt Hydrolysis & pH of Salt Solutions
umbrellas.description: Salts of weak acids or weak bases undergo hydrolysis in water, making the solution acidic or basic; predicting the pH direction requires identifying which ion reacts with water.

intro.label: Salt Hydrolysis Overview
intro.description: When a salt dissolves, its ions may react with water (hydrolyze): the conjugate base of a weak acid ($\text{A}^-$) accepts a proton to make OH⁻ (basic solution), and the conjugate acid of a weak base ($\text{BH}^+$) donates a proton to make H⁺ (acidic solution). Salts of strong acid + strong base give neutral solutions. The pH of a salt solution is calculated from the $K_a$ or $K_b$ of the hydrolyzing ion.

in_depth.label: Hydrolysis of the salt of a weak acid
in_depth.description: Predict that dissolving the salt of a weak acid (e.g., sodium acetate) gives a basic solution because the anion $\text{A}^-$ acts as a Brønsted base ($\text{A}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HA} + \text{OH}^-$), and calculate pH using $K_b = K_w/K_a$. This focuses on anionic hydrolysis to give OH⁻, not cationic hydrolysis (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Hydrolysis of the salt of a weak base
in_depth.description: Predict that dissolving the salt of a weak base (e.g., ammonium chloride) gives an acidic solution because the cation $\text{BH}^+$ donates a proton ($\text{BH}^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{B} + \text{H}^+$), and calculate pH using $K_a = K_w/K_b$. This focuses on cationic hydrolysis to give H⁺, not anionic hydrolysis (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Predicting the pH direction of salt solutions
in_depth.description: Classify any salt as acidic, basic, or neutral by identifying which ion (if any) hydrolyzes: strong acid + strong base → neutral; weak acid + strong base → basic; strong acid + weak base → acidic; weak acid + weak base → compare $K_a$ vs $K_b$ of hydrolysis. This focuses on qualitative pH direction, not quantitative calculation (separate keywords).
examples: weak–weak salt: compare $K_a$ vs $K_b$ to determine direction

umbrellas.label: Buffers & Henderson–Hasselbalch
umbrellas.description: A buffer resists pH change by containing a weak acid and its conjugate base in comparable amounts; the pH is calculated with the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation.

intro.label: Buffers and Henderson–Hasselbalch Overview
intro.description: A buffer solution contains a weak acid (HA) and its conjugate base (A⁻) at concentrations that are large and comparable, so the solution resists pH change when small amounts of strong acid or base are added. The pH is given by the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation: $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$. Common biological buffers include the carbonate ($\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3/\text{HCO}_3^-$) and phosphate ($\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-/\text{HPO}_4^{2-}$) systems.
examples: carbonate and phosphate: common biological buffers

in_depth.label: Buffer definition and resistance to pH change
in_depth.description: Define a buffer as a solution of a weak acid and its conjugate base (or weak base and its conjugate acid) that resists pH change by neutralizing added $\text{H}^+$ (conjugate base reacts) or added $\text{OH}^-$ (weak acid reacts). This focuses on the mechanism of buffering, not on the Henderson–Hasselbalch calculation (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Henderson–Hasselbalch equation
in_depth.description: Apply the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$ to calculate the pH of a buffer or determine the ratio $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]$ needed for a target pH. This focuses on the H–H calculation, not on the mechanism of buffering (a separate keyword).
examples: equal moles of HA and A⁻ → $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$

in_depth.label: Common buffer systems
in_depth.description: Identify the carbonate ($\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3/\text{HCO}_3^-$, blood pH ~7.4), phosphate ($\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-/\text{HPO}_4^{2-}$, intracellular), and acetate ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}/\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-$, laboratory) buffer systems and explain why each is suited to its pH range. This focuses on recognizing common systems, not on deriving the H–H equation (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Buffer capacity and effective range
in_depth.description: Predict that buffer capacity is greatest when $[\text{HA}] \approx [\text{A}^-]$ (i.e., pH = $\text{p}K_a$) and effective within $\text{p}K_a \pm 1$ pH unit; explain why excess strong acid or base overwhelms a buffer. This focuses on the capacity and range concept, not on the H–H calculation (a separate keyword).
examples: adding excess strong acid exhausts $[\text{A}^-]$, breaks buffer

umbrellas.label: Titration — Curves & Equivalence Points
umbrellas.description: A titration neutralizes an acid with a base (or vice versa); the titration curve tracks pH vs. volume added and reveals the equivalence point and, for weak-acid/base titrations, the half-equivalence point where $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$.

intro.label: Titration Curves Overview
intro.description: In an acid–base titration, a known concentration of base (or acid) is added from a burette to a solution of acid (or base) until stoichiometric equivalence is reached (the equivalence point). A plot of pH vs. volume of titrant is the titration curve; its shape reveals whether strong or weak acids/bases are involved. For weak acid–strong base titrations, the half-equivalence point occurs halfway to equivalence where exactly half the acid is neutralized and $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$.
examples: weak acid–strong base: equivalence at pH > 7

in_depth.label: Neutralization reaction
in_depth.description: Write the net ionic equation for neutralization ($\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$), calculate the moles of acid and base, and determine which reactant is limiting. This focuses on the stoichiometry of neutralization, not on reading the shape of a titration curve (a separate keyword).
examples: mol acid = mol base at equivalence

in_depth.label: Strong acid–strong base titration curve
in_depth.description: Interpret the titration curve for a strong acid titrated with a strong base: the pH rises slowly at first, then sharply at the equivalence point (pH = 7 at 25°C), then levels off. This focuses on the strong–strong curve shape and equivalence point pH, not on curves involving weak acids or bases (separate keywords).
examples: equivalence point at pH 7 (25°C) for strong–strong
before equivalence: pH set by excess strong acid

in_depth.label: Weak acid–strong base titration curve
in_depth.description: Interpret the titration curve for a weak acid titrated with a strong base: the initial pH is higher than a strong acid of equal concentration; a buffer region exists before equivalence; the equivalence point is at pH > 7 (salt hydrolysis); and a sharp inflection occurs at equivalence. This focuses on the weak-acid curve shape, not on the strong–strong curve (a separate keyword).
examples: buffer region: gradual slope before equivalence
start pH higher than strong acid of same molarity

in_depth.label: Equivalence point vs. half-equivalence point
in_depth.description: Identify the equivalence point (all acid neutralized; moles titrant added = moles analyte) and the half-equivalence point (half the acid is neutralized; $[\text{HA}] = [\text{A}^-]$ so $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$). This focuses on locating and interpreting both key points on a titration curve, not on calculating the pH at intermediate volumes (handled by H–H keyword).
examples: equivalence: all moles of acid reacted

in_depth.label: Buffer region on a titration curve
in_depth.description: Identify the buffer region on a weak-acid titration curve (between the start and equivalence point) as the region of gradual slope where the H–H equation applies, and explain that maximum buffer capacity occurs at the half-equivalence point. This focuses on recognizing the buffer region within the titration context, connecting to the H–H and buffer keywords.
examples: buffer region: flat slope between start and equivalence
steep inflection at equivalence marks end of buffer region

in_depth.label: Polyprotic acid titration curve
in_depth.description: Interpret a titration curve for a polyprotic acid (e.g., $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$, $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$): multiple equivalence points appear, one for each ionizable proton, each with its own half-equivalence point where $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_{an}$. This focuses on multi-equivalence-point curves, not on single-proton titrations (separate keywords).
examples: two equivalence points for a diprotic acid

umbrellas.label: Titration Indicators
umbrellas.description: Indicators are weak acids whose acid and conjugate-base forms have different colors; the correct indicator is chosen so its $\text{p}K_{in}$ is close to the titration's equivalence-point pH.

intro.label: Titration Indicators Overview
intro.description: An acid–base indicator is a weak acid ($\text{HIn}$) that has one color in its acid form and a different color as its conjugate base ($\text{In}^-$); the color change spans roughly $\text{p}K_{in} \pm 1$ pH unit. Choosing the right indicator means matching its transition range to the pH at the titration's equivalence point so the color change is sharp and unmistakable.
examples: endpoint = visible color change ≈ equivalence point

in_depth.label: How acid–base indicators work
in_depth.description: Explain that an indicator is itself a weak acid equilibrium ($\text{HIn} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{In}^-$) in which the acid and conjugate-base forms differ in color; the color shifts when pH crosses $\text{p}K_{in}$. This focuses on the indicator equilibrium mechanism, not on choosing which indicator to use for a specific titration (a separate keyword).

in_depth.label: Choosing the correct indicator
in_depth.description: Select an appropriate indicator for a titration by matching its transition pH range to the equivalence-point pH: strong–strong (~7) → bromothymol blue; weak acid–strong base (>7) → phenolphthalein; strong acid–weak base (<7) → methyl orange. This focuses on indicator selection criteria, not on the indicator mechanism (a separate keyword).
examples: match indicator range to equivalence pH

umbrellas.label: Redox Titration
umbrellas.description: A redox titration uses a standardized oxidizing or reducing agent to quantify a reducing or oxidizing analyte; the equivalence point is detected by color change or a potential jump, and stoichiometry is based on electron equivalents.

intro.label: Redox Titration Overview
intro.description: In a redox titration, the analyte is titrated with a standardized redox reagent (e.g., $\text{KMnO}_4$ or $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$) rather than an acid or base. The endpoint is detected by a color change of the titrant itself (self-indicating for $\text{KMnO}_4$) or an external indicator/potentiometer. Stoichiometry at equivalence uses electron equivalents (moles × electrons transferred), not merely mole ratios. The underlying redox bookkeeping (oxidation states, half-reactions, balancing) is handled by C4/C10; this keyword covers the titration technique and curve.
examples: equivalence: moles e⁻ donated = moles e⁻ accepted
redox titration curve plots potential vs. volume, not pH

in_depth.label: Redox titration technique and endpoint detection
in_depth.description: Describe how a redox titration works: a standardized oxidizing agent (e.g., $\text{KMnO}_4$) is added to the reducing analyte until all analyte is oxidized (equivalence point), detected by the permanent color of excess titrant (or an indicator). This focuses on the technique and endpoint detection, not on balancing the underlying half-reactions (C4/C10).
examples: endpoint = first permanent color that persists

in_depth.label: Redox titration stoichiometry
in_depth.description: Calculate the amount (moles) of analyte from a redox titration using electron-transfer stoichiometry: moles of oxidant × electrons per mole = moles of reductant × electrons per mole. This focuses on the quantitative calculation at equivalence, not on balancing the redox equation itself (C4).
examples: determine unknown concentration from volume × molarity of titrant
